item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, thank you! I don't know why I didn't catch that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, it stand for Miranda rights, another police cliché everyone knows from television.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
Edit: and now I realize that I posted under a years-old article. Sorry!

You're totally fine here—posting on old discussions isn't a problem if the comment contributes to the discussion meaningfully (as in, it's not just a spam comment like "lol" or "weird") and the conversation is still open for continuation (not something like a thread asking for help reviewing a draft that the author is no longer working on).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good concept - my main gripe is that I don't understand at all how the title ties into the anomaly. If its the same recurring victim, it isn't made clear.

Edit: and now I realize that I posted under a years-old article. Sorry!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What's the point? I see nothing at all anomalous here. Sounds like a work of pranksters.

they fully believe that a homicide has occurred and police investigation is under way, even when there is no corpse or police investigator at the site

Because they think it's a murder scene and assume that a homicide has occurred? How is this memetic in the skip-sense?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I dunno, initially upvoted, then came back and downvoted because I feel like it has no hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was forced to upvote this at spearpoint. Thanks Athena.

+ show block
– hide block

In all seriousness, I really enjoyed this, and it reminds me of cop cliches, which is a +1 in my book

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 2174 instances vary a lot: sometimes they look very much like the work of pranksters, sometimes they include many more convincing "props" - copious amount of genuine human blood, for example. And I'd figure murder is one of those things that can easily attract public attention - you only need, say, one tabloid picking up the story and sensationalising the hell out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm having a hard time understanding how the Foundation would come to see this as something in need of containment. It just seems like the kind of thing individual towns would simply dismiss as the work of pranksters. Am I missing something maybe? I'm curious.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I haven't seen Dexter, actually :P

My best guess is that there'd be a mix of Bones, CSI: Miami and a bit of CSI: Original in there. And a whole slew of other detectives, too, but those are the three specifically forensics shows I'm most familiar with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The addendum I could give or take, but the rest is interesting, at least mildly. I wouldn't call it one of my favorite SCPs in this contest, but I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay. This is definitely my kind of silly and weird!

Clever WTF basic idea, good writing, good fun, contrast between unexplainable object and A LOT OF explanations, the Foundation reporting on something it's never seen (now that is a first! Is it not?), do I detect too much influence of TV series here? Dexter among others? And the allusion to Buried Giant is always good, because Buried Giant is a masterpiece.

What clinched my upvote is the Cerne Abbas giant, though. WTF to the <lemniscate>th power!

My congratulations, Athena Grey! ;-D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
actually - in my mind, this is my little riff on Buried Giant, with its own moment of where the rug got pulled under you, where the story you thought you knew turn out to make absolutely no sense. Of course, if it didn't work then it didn't work.

It doesn't work for me either, sorry. It wasn't particularly interesting, and tried to take credit for a Cool Real Life thing. Whereas Buried Giant deals with the truly impossible-yet-mundane.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's quirky and engaging. I liked it.

Good job on your first scip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The bit about people making little shrines pushed me to upvote and I'm not sure why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe a Foundation supervisor is trying to pad his department's budget with a hoax SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree. The connection between the main phenomenon and the Cardiff giant feels a bit forced without more. I could like this if it were longer, or went in a different direction.

How does the Foundation know this is spontaneous, and not a prankster? Does it vary with local custom, in response to specific TV episodes?

Maybe it's the result of an incorporeal CSI fan, but not random. Maybe the investigating agents endangered themselves while researching it. Maybe the same phenomenon leads to anomalous parking tickets, based on popular culture's understanding - or anomalous taxes….

Maybe the responsible entity likes medical dramas or gets interested in the Free Software Movement, or hauntings …

item.title: Author's Note
item.content:encoded:
This is my first SCP and, at 493 words, my entry for the Short Works Contest. Many thanks to Baby_Boo, minmin, snorlison, Decibelle, Argenti, DolphinSlugchugger, Simulakyo, TyGently, vezaz and all the others who helped me with this (pretty sure I'm forgetting many names here; guys, give me a shout and I'll fix it).

I think I'm rather pleased at the result, although right now I'm probably too much in the afterglow to judge properly. There were all sort of lofty thematic goals: I wanted something like ophite’s Buried Giant with its “everything you know is wrong” moment in the Addendum. I wanted an anti-detective story – there’s the horror of murder, yes, but there’s no making sense of it at the end, no villain apprehended or motive explained…

Whether the execution met the goals? No clue. I might give a stab at rewriting this or that bit once the contest is over, though.

Image Credit: the page image is taken from Wikipedia's Chalk Outline page, and used here under the Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 2.0 Generic license. The image was created by Flickr user Tuválkin)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree. I feel like this article got cut off before you got to the hook.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Heh. In theory, the Addendum is supposed to be the curveball on this being "Mistaken police procedure brought to life", actually - in my mind, this is my little riff on Buried Giant, with its own moment of where the rug got pulled under you, where the story you thought you knew turn out to make absolutely no sense. Of course, if it didn't work then it didn't work.

I'll see if I can improve this bit after the contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one just doesn't grab me at all. It doesn't have anything 'wrong' with it, per se, but I found it completely uninteresting. There's no pay-off, no narrative, the reader is just left with 'spontaneous chalk body outlines' which isn't an anomaly that's interesting enough to keep me engaged. Since 'spontaneous chalk outlines' isn't that interesting, 'spontaneous chalk outlines in history' isn't an interesting ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having met you on the chat, I was excited to see your name on the short works list. I was not dispointed. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This feels like nothing more than "did you know that police don't actually outline bodies in chalk? it's true!" in SCP format.

I also don't care for when people write about things what are stereotypes, since 99% of the time there is no stereotypical instance of whatever it is the author is writing about. In this case, what would a "stereotypical" murder scene consist of? The presence of law enforcement authorities and a body? If that's what's being described, then describe that, instead of relying on a zero-information descriptive construct.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it should've been "anonymous".

(though I must admit, the idea of AWCY being responsible for chalk arts throughout history is cracking me up right now :P )

Edit: Wolfrover beat me to this. Thanks :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's odd to see the words "in real life" since it is supposed to be "in real life" in-universe. A better fit could be "in practice".

Hmm. I guess I was thinking in term of "real life" vs. "the popular culture belief" mentioned before that. But yeah… is this the real life? Is this just fantasy? I'll think of a better wording for after the contest.

If anything, I am grumbling over the extra info that we are denied of. This is an SCP Foundation version of a cocktease, if I may say so.

+ Just according to keikaku
- (Translator's note: keikaku means plan)

The cocktease is intentional :P Well, the idea is to let the audience feel the same frustration the Foundation must be having - there's a conspicuously empty hole in the middle of the SCP article, there's a conspicuously empty space in the middle of the SCP itself. Dunno whether I've struck the right balance, tho.

In any case, glad to hear you enjoyed it. And thanks for the feedback :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Initially no-voted due to some vague perception of a lack of 'meat' to this, then switched to upvote at the notion of Cerne Abbas. Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The amount of detail and imagery present in said detail is incredible, and I applaud you for not only making it work in so little words, but for also doing it as your first skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like there's way too much detail in here, and the end result is, what exactly? Mistaken police procedure brought to life? It's just not interesting in the least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Elegant, clean, well written. Tone is excellent, and I can believe in the existence of this SCP.

Only glitch:

2. “SCP-2174 instances” in this document refers to both confirmed and alleged ones. For a detailed discussion of suspected false positives (works by unanimous pranksters etc.), see Document Alpha-DXL, Section F

Now, is this being done by a collective of pranksters that are in agreement, or by anonymous pranksters?

Beyond that +1. Very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

In real life, such practice contaminates the scene and is only done by uninformed officers acting due to a mistaken understanding of procedure.

It's odd to see the words "in real life" since it is supposed to be "in real life" in-universe. A better fit could be "in practice".

Pros:

  • I love the degree of elaboration in this article.
  • The addendum brings a fine degree of depth into the article.

Cons:

  • If anything, I am grumbling over the extra info that we are denied of. This is an SCP Foundation version of a cocktease, if I may say so.

Verdict: Upvote
